claim: The minimum viable ledger record has exactly 12 fields: record_id, verb, source_project, source_path, dest_project, dest_path, source_version, moved_at, unit_type, scope_tag, verdict, ledger_schema_version. All four movement verbs (port, sync-back, extract, context-sync) can be served by these 12 fields.

The lineage ledger must be an append-only event log, not a mutable record design. /sync-back's three-way diff demands the original port record be preserved intact. Current state for a (source_project, source_path, dest_project) tuple is derivable as the most-recent record for that tuple.

The parent_record_id field is unnecessary in an append-only log. Temporal ordering plus the identity tuple (source_project, source_path, dest_project) provides the back-reference without a dedicated field.
The status field is redundant in an append-only log. Event ordering implies current state; a mutable status field in an immutable log is a contradiction.

The notes field (free-text catch-all) must be rejected from the ledger. The G.1 lesson: agents that cannot populate it set it to null universally, making it a landing pad for information that should have been structured or not stored.

git-subrepo is a negative example for ledger design: its mutable single-record .gitrepo block cannot answer what a subrepo looked like three syncs ago, and it loses the upstream connection silently when the upstream URL changes.

Nix derivation immutability (each .drv is permanent and immutable; lineage built by following inputDrvs chain) is the correct positive analogue for ledger design: each record is permanent, ancestry is the chain of records.

The ledger edge model should be a forward pointer (destination knows its source) plus content hash at port time. This gives: where-did-this-come-from (forward pointer), has-source-changed (hash comparison), no writes into source repos, no consistency hazard.
Bidirectional ledger pointers (source knows destination AND destination knows source) create consistency hazards when one side updates and the other does not. Not recommended for JASON-OS.

Repo-evolution events (rename, file split, file merge, delete) do not invalidate ledger records — they mean the record describes a past state. The source_status field (active | source_deleted | source_renamed | standalone) is the resolution mechanism.
For repo-rename events, the fix-up is a bulk update (find all records with source_repo = old_name, rewrite to new_name) triggered when the orchestrator detects a 404 on any ledger pointer resolution.

Multi-hop transitive edges should use one record per hop, not full ancestry chains in a single record. Ancestry is the chain of ledger records. At JASON-OS's realistic depth (2-3 hops), recursive lookup cost is trivial.
The lineage ledger should live at .claude/state/ledger.jsonl — the existing gitignored state directory. This matches the convention of all six existing .claude/state/*.jsonl files.

The ledger must NOT be git-tracked. JSONL produces real merge conflicts on independent appends; no JSONL merge driver auto-resolves. Git tracking also raises CLAUDE.md §2 security constraints for path/content data.

safe-fs.withLock (coarse whole-file) is the correct locking primitive for ledger writes. Writes are short (microseconds), nested companion invocations are sequential in practice, 5s timeout is generous, and stale locks are auto-broken via PID liveness check.

The ledger record must be the last step of a movement — files-first, then ledger append. If any file write fails before the ledger append, the ledger is untouched (no rollback needed). If the ledger append fails after all files are written, the result is an orphaned movement (detectable on next scan), not torn state.

Cross-machine sync of the ledger file is handled by /context-sync, not by git. The ledger is a user-scoped artifact that /context-sync includes in its managed set. This is architecturally clean — the ledger sync is a direct use of the tool being built.

Single ledger file is appropriate at launch; sharding threshold is approximately 2,000 records (roughly 3-5 years of heavy use at estimated rates). Year-based sharding (ledger.2026.jsonl, ledger.2027.jsonl) is the right strategy if reached.

The ledger schema versioning should mirror the existing JASON-OS EVOLUTION.md approach: per-record ledger_schema_version stamp, addition with a default = minor bump, addition requiring re-reading existing records = major bump with migration plan.

The EVOLUTION.md §8 mirror rule (ledger schema must be mirrored identically to SoNash) does NOT apply to the lineage ledger. The ledger tracks movements between repos and lives in one canonical location.

/context-sync does NOT need the full lineage ledger. Lineage is universally unneeded for context-sync — all brainstorm-listed source categories are user-authored in place, none ported from an external repo. A separate 7-field drift record is sufficient and minimal.

For an owned target repo, the signal-carrying files that profile discovery must read are: .github/workflows/*.yml (static YAML parse), .husky/pre-commit and pre-push (heuristic-grep), .claude/settings.json (JSON parse), .claude/hooks/*.js (header-read + classification), .github/CODEOWNERS (line-by-line parse), .github/pull_request_template.md (heuristic extraction), and .gitignore (line-by-line for write-exclusion zones).
The .husky/_/ subdirectory is auto-generated by Husky v9 and marked DEPRECATED in its own husky.sh file. It carries no user-authored gate signal and must be skipped in profile extraction.

settings.local.json is gitignored and machine-local. Profile discovery against a remote clone without a local checkout will miss it. The profile must document this gap explicitly as not discoverable remotely.

JASON-OS settings.json deny list blocks four patterns: Bash(git push --force *), Bash(git push origin main), Bash(git reset --hard *), Bash(rm -rf *). Any companion operating in JASON-OS must not emit these commands in Bash tool calls.

A target-process-profile carries exactly 8 top-level fields: schema_version, repo_name, repo_remote_url, discovered_at, discovered_at_sha, discovery_source_map, gates (array), shapes (object). No fields beyond operational necessity.

Each gate record in the profile carries exactly 6 fields: gate_id, trigger, required_by, action_description, companion_directive, confidence. The companion_directive field is operationally critical — it is the field the moving companion reads as an instruction.

The shapes section uses exactly 3 fields per unit type (directory, companion_files, naming_scheme) for five unit types: file, family, memory, context-artifact, concept. This is the Challenge 6 resolution from the BRAINSTORM made concrete.

Target-process-profiles live at .claude/state/profiles/<repo-name>.json inside JASON-OS — not inside the target repo itself (chicken-and-egg), and not in the root of .claude/state/ (separate from other state files as the set of tracked repos grows).

Profile discovery for unowned (read-only) repos uses bare clone plus git-show reads (Option B). git clone --bare --no-local <source> <scratch>; reads via git show HEAD:<path>. Makes writes into the unowned source structurally impossible — no working-tree paths exist on disk.

safe-fs.isSafeToWrite should be extended with a forbiddenRoots parameter as defense-in-depth for unowned repos. The check must use /^\.\.([\/]|$)/ regex per CLAUDE.md §5 anti-patterns (not startsWith('..') which is the anti-pattern).

Unowned-mode profiles differ from owned-mode profiles in four ways: location (unowned/ subdirectory), discovery_mode field (unowned-static), probe_eligible field (false), and all signal confidence capped at observed-only (not verified).

The confirmed /context-sync source inventory has 18 categories after splitting mixed-scope items and adding items missing from the brainstorm. Key additions: user-level agents (~/.claude/agents/), project-level agents (.claude/agents/), status-line config.local.toml split from config.toml, git config section-level scope split, ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (reserved), IDE keybindings (optional/advisory).

The /context-sync drift detection algorithm: (1) load drift records into in-memory map, (2) build machineExcludeSet, (3) for each category, enumerate units via glob, (4) skip machine-excluded units, (5) normalize and hash both sides, (6) classify into 5 states (NEW/CLEAN/SOURCE-DRIFTED/DEST-DRIFTED/BOTH-DRIFTED), (7) propose non-CLEAN units to user with diff, (8) on confirmation write file then drift record.

Context-sync must hash normalized content, not raw bytes. Four normalizations: (1) CRLF-to-LF for all text files (default-on, Windows routine problem), (2) RFC 8785 JCS-style deep key sort for all .json files, (3) strip volatile frontmatter keys (last-modified, synced_at, last-touched, updated_at) for .md files in memory/tenet/claude-md categories, (4) comment-only changes treated as semantic (not stripped).

Cross-machine locale handling uses a hybrid of two mechanisms: Layer 1 (per-record machine_exclude boolean in the drift record, set at first-sync for known-risky categories) plus Layer 2 (content-time pattern detection using the existing sanitize-error.cjs battery, surfaces MACHINE-BOUND-DETECTED state, user decides sync-anyway / skip / always-skip).

The scope-tag enum must NOT be extended with a sixth value for the machine-bound-but-labeled-user case. The existing machine scope tag already covers purely machine-bound items. A static enum value cannot encode the runtime detection problem for partially-portable items. Enum stays at five values.

The ~/.claude/projects/<hash>/ directory name encodes the project's absolute path, making it structurally different per machine. Resolution: walk ~/.claude/projects/ empirically for a subdirectory whose memory/ folder contains a known filename. If not found (new machine, project never opened), create the directory with confirmation.

The /context-sync decide loop is a proposal loop — no writes happen without confirmation. CLEAN units skip silently. NEW and SOURCE-DRIFTED units require per-unit yes/no with diff preview. DEST-DRIFTED depends on source_wins field. BOTH-DRIFTED requires mandatory individual resolution and cannot be bulk-resolved.
The comprehension cache key is: unit_type:source_repo_id:target_repo_id:profile_slice_hash. Four fields, colon-delimited. This is the structural identity of the movement, not the content identity.

The unit_content_hash must NOT be part of the fast-path cache key. Including it would cause the fast path to miss on every file edit, making it useless for actively-developed sources. Content hash belongs in the cache value as a per-entry staleness check.

The profile_slice_hash must hash only the profile fields that recipes actually consult (gate flags vector + shape conventions for this unit type), not the full profile object. Including irrelevant profile fields causes spurious cache misses — the same anti-pattern Bazel's explicit --action_env allowlist was created to solve.

Four cache invalidation triggers: (1) source unit content changes (lazy per-entry staleness check via content_hash_at_last_verdict), (2) target profile re-discovered with different fingerprint (partial invalidation by target_repo_id), (3) recipe library version bump (global invalidation), (4) manual user override (force_recomprehend flag skips lookup).

The comprehension cache lives at .claude/state/comprehension-cache.jsonl — separate from the ledger. Ledger records lineage (what happened). Cache records recipe applicability (what pattern applies). Coupling these produces schema pollution and incorrect query semantics.

Fast-path cache hits must surface in the dashboard before any action is taken — they are not executed silently. The fast path is a mode of the dashboard, not a bypass of it. Confidence below 0.8 suppresses the fast path even on a key hit.

The always-last-step rule applies to all four companions: state records (ledger record, drift record, cache entry) are always the final write in a movement operation. If pre-record writes fail, the record is untouched. If the record write fails, the result is an orphan detectable on next scan.

The 7-field drift record pattern is the JASON-OS house style for lightweight state. All six existing .claude/state/*.jsonl files are pure event logs (timestamp + categorical fields + outcome). The lineage ledger (12 fields) is more complex because it genuinely needs more structure; the drift record fits the established house pattern.

Schema versioning mirrors across all four data structures (ledger, profile, drift record, comprehension cache). All independently converged on per-record schema_version stamping using the EVOLUTION.md approach. The planner should adopt a single naming convention and write one shared schema-versioning policy document.

The no-fire-and-forget principle applies across all four strands. Fast-path hits surface in the dashboard (D11). Context-sync CLEAN state skips silently but all other states require confirmation (D8). The ledger-last rule prevents silent partial-state writes (D3). This aligns with CLAUDE.md §4 rule 6 and the BRAINSTORM anti-goal.

The security pattern battery (sanitize-error.cjs: /C:\\Users\\[^\\]+/gi, /\/home\/[^/\s]+/gi, /\/Users\/[^/\s]+/gi) is directly reusable across three strands: context-sync content-time detection (D10), pre-sync secrets scanning (D9), and unowned-mode forbiddenRoots guard (D7). No new pattern design is needed.

Mixed-scope splitting is required at the item level, not the category level. Canonical memories are per-file scope (type frontmatter field determines scope). settings.local.json mixes portable and machine-bound entries in the same JSON array. Global git config mixes identity (user) with credential helpers (machine) in the same file. Sub-file granularity for the JSON array case is an unresolved planning decision.

chezmoi uses SHA256 hashes stored in a persistent bucket (not raw content) to track dotfile state across invocations. This directly validates the src_hash/dst_hash field choice in the A4 drift record schema.

CRLF-vs-LF is a documented real false-positive vector in chezmoi on Windows (discussion #3816). Even chezmoi had a directive-name bug that caused line-ending normalization to silently fail. Context-sync must make CRLF normalization default-on, not opt-in.

JSON key ordering produces different SHA256 hashes for semantically identical documents. RFC 8785 JCS specifically exists to make JSON hashing stable across key-order variation. Context-sync must apply deep key sort + compact serialization before hashing any .json unit.

No dotfile manager surveyed (chezmoi, yadm, Syncthing, home-manager) provides automated heuristic detection of machine-bound content without explicit user opt-in. Both chezmoi and yadm require explicit user-authored conditionals or class labels. Context-sync's Layer 2 (content-time detection) is novel — no prior art to validate against.

~/.claude/statusline/config.local.toml contains a confirmed live API key (weather_api_key). This file must be set to machine_exclude: true at category registration time, not discovered at first-run. It is the canonical example for machine-scoped-by-design category handling.

settings.local.json in JASON-OS contains both portable and machine-bound entries in the same JSON array (e.g., generic Bash(chmod +x *) alongside machine-specific C:/Users/jason/... paths). The correct sync unit is the subset of portable entries, not the whole file — requiring sub-file iteration for JSON arrays.

PR #10 R2 CRITICAL (commit 088a077) fixed 782 absolute path references across 60 committed research files via an after-the-fact one-shot transformer. No pre-commit gate exists to prevent future leaks. Context-sync's content-time detection (Layer 2) would catch the same patterns at sync time.

The bootstrap paradox: /context-sync must ship first as the bootstrap scaffold. Forcing it to depend on the lineage ledger (which is designed in parallel) inverts the dependency and prevents /context-sync from being the simplest first companion. This is a definitive argument for the separate drift record.

User-level agents at ~/.claude/agents/ are distinct from project-level agents at .claude/agents/. Both directories are confirmed present. ~/.claude/agents/ contains 12 GSD agents; .claude/agents/ contains JASON-OS research/workflow agents. Both should be separate rows in the /context-sync inventory.

Project-level skills in .claude/skills/ belong to the /port companion (require the understanding layer and transformation work), NOT to /context-sync. Including them in /context-sync would be scope leakage.

The memory type frontmatter field (user, feedback, project, reference, tenet, index from enums.json) provides a natural scope-classification mechanism for canonical memory files. If the memory_type-to-scope-tag mapping is defined once, the walk uses it automatically without per-file manual classification.

No file currently uses type: tenet in frontmatter. The one confirmed tenet file (t3_convergence_loops.md) uses type: reference. Tenet identification is currently by naming convention (t3_ prefix or t[N]_ prefix), not by frontmatter type field — contrary to what BRAINSTORM assumed.

A two-level cache design (structural key + content-hash validation) mirrors proven build system designs: Bazel uses action cache + content-addressable store; ccache direct mode uses manifest + include-hash validation. Both separate routing (the key) from freshness checking (the value).

Recipe library version should be a global cache invalidator at v1 scale (Turborepo pattern for configuration-level changes). Per-recipe versioning is the upgrade path if the library grows to 10+ distinct unit_type recipe sets.

SPDX 3.0.1 stores relationships as separate Relationship element records with from and to properties — not as inline pointers within element records. This validates the forward-pointer-plus-separate-edge-record design space, though JASON-OS uses simpler inline forward pointers within the 12-field cap.

Content hash alone cannot represent derived-from relationships once content diverges. The relationship must be explicitly recorded at port time. This confirms that source_content_hash in the ledger record is a drift-detection tool, not a substitute for the forward pointer.

git log --follow uses content-similarity heuristics (default 50% threshold) for rename detection but fails when files are heavily modified during a rename/move. Content hash at port time provides a fallback for searching the source repo for a file with the same hash when the path has changed.

Node.js v22 Permission Model (--permission --allow-fs-read/write) has a known symlink bypass documented in the official API reference — symlinks are followed even outside granted paths. This makes it insufficient as a sole enforcement layer for unowned-repo sandboxing.

The fastest-path hit rate and verdict divergence rate are the observable quality signals for cache key tuning. A well-tuned key produces >60% hit rate for common file types after the first few ports. Frequent divergence (cached verdict != re-evaluated verdict) signals the profile_slice_hash is not capturing enough relevant profile structure.

The existing scope-tag enum (universal/user/project/machine/ephemeral) applies to the lineage ledger without modification. This enum was validated across five independent systems in PHASE_0_LANDSCAPE.md.
safeAtomicWriteSync (tmp + rename) is NOT appropriate for ledger appends — it rewrites the whole file and is expensive for a growing log. Use safeAppendFileSync inside withLock instead.

Torn lines from mid-write crashes are non-fatal: safeParseLine in parse-jsonl-line.js returns null for blank or malformed lines; readJsonl warns and skips them. Outcome is an unregistered movement (orphan), caught by orphan detection on next scan.

JASON-OS has no ESLint, Prettier, or local lint configs checked in. Style enforcement runs via SonarCloud (CI) rather than local pre-commit lint. Ported code will surface in SonarCloud review but will not block commits locally.

run-node.sh enforces HOOKS_DIR confinement: hook scripts must be under .claude/hooks/, no symlinks, no .. path segments. Hook files ported from another repo must land in .claude/hooks/ or be rejected at execution time.

GitHub Actions workflows in JASON-OS use SHA-pinned actions throughout. New workflow files ported into JASON-OS should follow the same SHA-pinning convention to match the repo's supply-chain hygiene pattern.

Branch protection is not enabled at the GitHub API level for JASON-OS main. The push-to-main block is enforced solely via the Husky pre-push hook and the block-push-to-main.js Claude Code PreToolUse hook. CI outcomes are informational, not blocking at the platform level.

The profile's discovered_at_sha enables staleness detection without re-reading all source files. A SHA mismatch between stored and current HEAD triggers re-discovery. This is a standard content-addressed cache invalidation pattern used in GitHub Actions caching and Terraform state.

Husky _shared.sh (the hand-authored shared lib with SKIP_CHECKS helpers and EXIT trap chaining) is a separate sync-eligible artifact from .husky/_/ (the generated Husky v9 shims). The latter is ephemeral and must never be synced.

Per-repo .git/config is NOT a /context-sync target. It contains hooksPath, remote URLs, and branch tracking — all machine+project specific. Syncing it would overwrite machine-specific configuration with another machine's values.
No file in any memory directory currently uses type:tenet frontmatter. t3_convergence_loops.md uses type:reference. tenet is schema-valid but uninstantiated.
The only working tenet identification mechanism is the t[N]_ filename prefix. type:tenet frontmatter does not function because no file uses it.
~/.claude/projects/ directory names are reversible path-encodings not hashes. Algorithm: replace colon with double-dash, replace path separators with single dash.
Encoded project directory name is machine-local. Different absolute paths on different machines produce different encoded names. Use git remote URL as stable cross-machine identifier.
No project.json manifest exists inside ~/.claude/projects/<encoded>/ to record the original absolute path. Mapping must be inferred from encoding algorithm or maintained externally.
Canonical MCP config is ~/.claude.json (home root), NOT ~/.claude/mcp.json. The mcp.json.bak is a migration artifact from the old format.
Project-scoped MCP servers live in .mcp.json at project root (version-controlled). JASON-OS has no .mcp.json at its project root.
~/.claude.json requires its own sync-unit row in the context-sync inventory. Not currently listed in D9. Scope-tag: machine (project-path keys differ per machine, potential credentials).
Path-encoding algorithm for ~/.claude/projects/ names: replace colon with double-dash, replace backslash or forward-slash with single dash. Deterministic and reversible.
chezmoi has no built-in ephemeral-host concept. Canonical pattern is user-authored ephemeral boolean detecting CODESPACES and REMOTE_CONTAINERS_IPC env-vars plus username heuristics.
CI=true is set universally by GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI. No major dotfiles manager natively reads it as an ephemeral signal. Inferrable from chezmoi env template but not formalized.
Cheapest JASON-OS v1 ephemeral guard: CI=true OR CODESPACES=true then exit 0. Covers CI runners and Codespaces without per-machine config. Approximately 95 percent of ephemeral cases.
home-manager has no bail-on-unknown-hostname feature. Unknown hostname produces evaluation error. Community pattern for CI: dedicated homeConfigurations.ci flake entry or skip activation.
GNU Stow has no ephemeral detection. All CI and ephemeral logic must live in the caller shell script. stow-local-ignore must be maintained manually per machine.
VS Code settingsSync.ignoredSettings is a global denylist (itself synced), not per-machine. Issue 89627 requesting per-machine local ignore has been open since 2020 with no resolution.
chezmoi modify_ template plus setValueAtPath provides per-key machine-specific JSON patching but requires chezmoi as dependency and per-file template authoring. Not recommended for JASON-OS v1.

Cheapest v1 per-key portability for settings.local.json: gitignored sidecar file settings.local.machine.json following Docker Compose override model. No schema changes, about 10 lines Node.js. Sidecar wins on conflict.

Second-cheapest v1 sub-file JSON key portability: gitignored JSON Pointer denylist (RFC 6902 paths) listing entries to protect from sync. Keeps settings.local.json as single file with per-key granularity. About 25 lines Node.js.
